California
Fair Political
Practices Commrsslon
December 10, 199O
Samuel SiegeJ., city Attorney
office of the citY Attorney
4909 Lakewood Boulevard, Suite 3oo
Laker^/ood, CA 9O7 L2
Re: Your Reguest for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-682
Dear ltr. siegel:
This letter responds to your reguest for advice regarding the
duties and respons ilitities 
of city of La Puente councilmenber
Louis R. Perez and Planning Commissioner Arthur Gutierrez under
the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the ttAct").1 Your request for advice is of a general nature and
does not refer to a particular decision before the officials' 
Ac-
cordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal as-
s i stan6e- pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329(c) (copy
enclosed).2
OUESTION
Do the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act disqualify
Council,member perez and Planning Cornrnissioner Gutierrez frorn
oarticipatins in decisions regarding the adoptj'on of a redevelop-
irent ptin roi the city of La Puente?
CONCLUS ION
The conf lict-of- interest provisions of the Act disgualify
councilmember Perez and Pl-anning connissioner Gutierrez fron
participati,ng in redevelopment decisions if such decisions will
ira.re . toreseeable material effect upon their economic interests.
1 cor".n*"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A1I statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
commi-ssion
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations. Section
18ooo, et seq. Al1 references to regulations are to Title 2,
oivision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 rnform.l assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322-5660


FACTS
The city of La Puente is currently in the process of adopting
a redevelopment plan for the downtown business district. 
The
members of the La Puente city council also serve as the La Puente
Redevelopment Agency. You are the city attorney for the City of
La Puente. In that capacity you seek our advice regarding the
potentially disqualifyinq financial interests of councilmember
Louis R. perez ind Planning commissioner Arthur Gutierrez.
Councilmember Petez' spouse holds a part-time position in a
srna1l retail 
$romen's dress store located within the redevelopment
project area. She has received salary from her employer in excess
of 5250 t/ithin the Past Year.
Planning Commissioner Arthur Gutierrez owns his place of
res j,dence which is within 3oo feet of the boundary of a redevelop-
ment project which would upgrade an old downtown area. The
p.opeity-to be redeveloped is all- zoned doltntown business
ii=irict. 
This project would not affect the zoning or use of the
property owned bY Mr. Gutj'errez-
Comrnissioner Gutierrez' residence is located on a lot $'hich
is currently zoned R-1 but which is designated rnultiple-fanily
residentiaL high density in the general pIan. The lot is 1l-,160
square feet. 
Wi.thin the City of La Puente, 12 percent of the
r6sidential lots are equal to or larger than that of llr'
Gutj.errez. The highest and best use of the property would be for
the construction oi three rental units. 
Mr' Gutierrez has no
plans to build rental unj.ts on his lot and intends to continue to
ieside in his single-fanily residence.
ANALYSTS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
fron making,
participating in, oi using their officiat position to influence a
lovernrnLntal decision in which they know or have reason to knor.r
€hey have a financiat interest. 
Members of the city council and
planning conmissioners are public officials. 
(Section 82048')
An official 
has a financial- interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable. that the
decisi5n wil-1 have a material financial effect, distinguishable
frorn its effect on the public generally, on the official 
or on a
,".r"r 
of the officiaf i inrnediate faurily,3 or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
Fi■e No. ■-90-682
Page 2
3  An Officia■
′s ''immediate fami■yl' are his or her spouse and
dependent chi■dren.  (SeCtion 82029。
)


File No. 
■-90-682
Page 3
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,0oo) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution 
j.n the regular course of business on
terms available to the Public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty doI-
l-ars ($25o) or nore in value provided to, received
by or pronised to the public official vrithin 12
m6nths prior to the time lrhen the decision is nade.
(d) Any business entj.ty in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
ernployee, or holds any position of nanagement.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
t*o hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or pronised to the public
offici"f 
within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is nade.
For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official , by an agent on behalf of a publ-ic of-
ficial,. or Ly a business entity or trust in vrhich
the official, 
the official's 
agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, 
or
beneficially a lo-percent interest or greater.
Section 87103.
The spouse of Councilmember Perez is an employee of a retail
store localed $rithin the redevelopnent project area. For purPoses
of the Act, the income of an individual includes any cornmunity
property j-nterest in the income of a spouse. (Section 82030.)
tn"i"foi", 
assuming that Mrs. Perez has received $500 or nore in
incorne fron the retail store in the preceding twelve nonths, the
retaj,l store nay be a disqualifying economic interest, as defined
in Section 87103(c), for councilnenber Perez.
You have also advised us that Planning Commissioner Gutierrez
owns a residence located within 300 feet of the boundary of a
redevel-opment project area. For purPoses of our analysis, we as-
sume that Mr. cutierrez' interest is valued at $1,000 or more.
Adoption of a redevelopment plan nay affect the economic
interest! of these public officials. 
consequently, Councj.lmernber
Perez and Planning Comrnissioner Gutierrez nust disqualify
themselves from participating in decisions concerning the


File No. I-90-682
Page 4
redevelopment plan if the decisions will foreseeably and materi-
a1ly affect their interests in a nanner that is distinguishable
frorn the effect of the decisions on the public generally.
Foreseeab i l itv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial l-ikelihood that they ti11 occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision nust be more than a mere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. 
(Downev cares v. Downev
Cornmunitv Development Comm. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983, 989-99Li
witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; fn re Thorner (1975)
L FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed). ) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conf L j.ct of interest. 
(witt v. UqE-E-gIq,
supra at 823. )
one of the major goal-s of a redevelopment plan is to increase
property values, in particular within the project area and. Less
ai.Lctfy within the entire community. Any decision. regarding the
redevelopnent plan wiI1, foreseeably, have a financial effect on
business and real estate investments in the redeveloPment area.
(Do$/nev Cares v. Dolrnev CommunitY DeveloDBent comm., suDra; In re
oqfesbv (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71; Sieqel Advice Letter, No. I-90-650,
copies encl-osed.) Redevelopment projects have a long tern effect
on the value of ]and as well as on the business development
potential of the area. Accordingly, decisions concerning the
idoption of a redevelopnent Plan and the revitalization of the
downtown business area will- foreseeably have an irnpact on the
retail store which is a source of income to councilmember Perez.
Renovation of the downtown area is likeIy to attract an increased
number of potentiaf custoners and lead to an increase in saLes'
such decisions will also have a foreseeable effect on Planning
Commissioner cutierrez' residence which is Located rrithin 300 feet
of the redevelopment project. 
The renovation of the dolrntown area
is likely to leid to an increase in the vaLue of property in the
immed j.ate vicinity.
Materi,alitv
once it has been determined that it is reasonably foreseeable
that adoptj.on of a redevelopment PIan wil'] have a financial' effect
on a source of income and on the real property interests of the
public officials, 
we rnust next determine whether the effect will
be naterial. 
Regulation 18702 (copy encJ.osed) sets forth the
guidelines for determining whether an official's 
financial inter-
est in a decision is lmaterial" as required by Section 87103. ' If
the officj-a1's f j.nancial j-nterest is directly involved in the
decision, then Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) applies to
deternine rnateriality. 
If on the other hand the official's 
inter-
est would be indirectly affected by the decision, then Regulations
L8702-2 through ]-8702.6 (copies enclosed) would apply to deterrnine
whether the effect of the decision is material.


File No. l-90-642
Page 5
councilmember Perez
The retait business which has been a source of incorne to
councj.lmember Perez' spouse will be affected indirectly by deci-
sions to adopt a redevelopment plan. I{hen a business entity which
is a source of income to a public official 
is indirectly affected
by a decision, the aPplicable standards for determining material-
ily are set forth in Regulation 18702.2. For al-I but the largest
bu-siness concerns, the applicable standard is that of subdivision
(S) of Regul,ation La7o2.2 nhich states j.n pertinent Part that a
decision is rnateriaL if:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision wiII result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscat year in the amount of 12,500 or nore; or
(3) The decision wiLL result in an increase
or d,ecrease in the value of assets or l-iabilities
of S1o,00o or more.
Regulation ta7 02 .2 (g) .
If the projected adoption of a redevelopment plan will affect
this retait buslness vhich is a source of income to Councilmember
Perez' spouse in the amounts indicated above, disqua lification 
is
required.
Planninq conmissioner Gutierrez
Planning Comrnissioner Gutierrez owns a residence situated
within 3oo feet of a proposed redeveloprnent project which will
upgrade an ol-d downtown area. The effect of a decision is rnate-
riit 
as to real property in which an official 
has a direct
indj.rect or beneiici,al ownership interest (not including a
leasehold interest), if any of the following applies:
(1) The real, property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that reaL Property,
is Located within a 3oo foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision, un-
less the decision will have no financial effect
upon the officj.al's real property interest.
Regulation ta702.3 (a) (1).
Adoption of a redevetopnent plan rrhich includes a project for
upgrading the downtown area is presumed to have a material
financial effect upon property located within 300 feet of the


File No. I-9o-6a2
Page 6
project. 
Thus, Planning Commissioner Gutierrez must disqualify
frirnsef f from participating in such decisj'ons unless he can
demonstrate that the decisions !rj.11 have no financial effect upon
his residence.
Public cenerallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disqual ification is required only if the
effect of the decision is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. 
(section 87103.) If the decision does not af-
iect all rnernberi of the public in the same manner, disgualifica-
tion may be required unless the effect of the decisj'on on the
public offj.cial's 
interest is the same as the effect on a
!ignificant 
segment of the pub}ic. 
(Regulation 18703, copy
enclosed. )
For purposes of applying the public Aenerally exception, we
must compire the effecl oi tne decisions on lots situated within
3oo feet or less of the project and which are similar in size to
the lot owned by Planning cornmissioner Gutierrez. 
(Hentschke
Advice Letter, No. A-90-420, copy enclosed.) we have insufficient
information to deterrnine whether a significant segiment of the
resj,dential Lots in the jurisdiction are sirnilarly situated.
Hor.rever, we need not reach the issue at this tine. 
You have
advised us that only 12 percent of the lots in the jurisdiction
are equal in area or larger than Conmissioner Gutierrez' Iot' 
As
we haie previously advised you, redevel-opment decisj-ons tend to
have a g-reater efiect on larger lots. 
(Siecrel Advice Letter, No'
A-90-50i, copy enclosed.) Therefore, we conclude that the public
generally exleption does not apply.to Planning Commissioner
6utierreZ and he rnust dj.squalify hirnself from participating in any
decision regarding the redevelopment of the old downtol^'n area
which is siiuated within 3oo feet of his resi-dence unless he can
show that such decisions will have no financial effect upon his
residence as discussed above.
conversely, if the redevelopnent area has already been
adopted and the city is now in the process of adopting specific
project area p1ans, these decisions may be capable-of segTmentation
i., i ...,.,". which may pennit !tr. Gutierrez to participate in sorne
decisions. The applicable standards are those of Requlation
7a702.3 for purposes of determining the rnateriality of a decision.
For exampLe, if the distance between Mr- Gutierrez' residence and
the s j,te of a particular decj.sion is between 300 and 2,500 feet,
the effect of I decision wift be deemed rnateriaf if the decision
will have a financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,000) or nore on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official 
has an interest; or


(3) Wi■■ affect the renta■
 va■ue of the
property by S■′000 。r more per ■2 month per■od.
Regu■ation ■8702.3(a)(3)(A)and (B)。
To ass■st you 
■n determin■ng whether particular decis■
ons may
be segmented′  We are enc■osing Booqaard Advice Letter′
 No.
I-90-347.  ShOu■d you have any further questions regarding this
matter′  do not hesitate to Cal■
 me at (9■6)322-590■
.

sincerely′

Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322-5660 FACTS The city of La Puente is currently in the process of adopting a redevelopment plan for the downtown business district. The members of the La Puente city council also serve as the La Puente Redevelopment Agency. You are the city attorney for the City of La Puente. In that capacity you seek our advice regarding the potentially disqualifyinq financial interests of councilmember Louis R. perez ind Planning commissioner Arthur Gutierrez. Councilmember Petez' spouse holds a part-time position in a srna1l retail $romen's dress store located within the redevelopment project area. She has received salary from her employer in excess of 5250 t/ithin the Past Year. Planning Commissioner Arthur Gutierrez owns his place of res j,dence which is within 3oo feet of the boundary of a redevelop- ment project which would upgrade an old downtown area. The p.opeity-to be redeveloped is all- zoned doltntown business ii=irict. This project would not affect the zoning or use of the property owned bY Mr. Gutj'errez- Comrnissioner Gutierrez' residence is located on a lot $'hich is currently zoned R-1 but which is designated rnultiple-fanily residentiaL high density in the general pIan.
